title: Chapter 57: Splinter
content: The first sign that the night was going to get worse came at 1:47 AM.
I was on the eastern wall, checking the fortifications with Colt, when the radio crackled with Pratt's voice—harsh, urgent, and tinged with something that sounded like betrayal.
"Colt. We have a problem. Vance and Kowalski are gone."
Colt went still. "Gone how?"
"They took weapons and ammo. Slipped out the northeast exit fifteen minutes ago. Harmon tried to stop them—Vance pulled a knife."
"How bad?"
"Harmon's cut. Not deep. But they're gone, and they took two rifles, four hundred rounds, and the portable generator."

Colt's jaw clenched so hard I could hear his teeth grind. The Command System flickered on his wrist—data processing, threat assessment, personnel tracking. Whatever it showed him made his expression go flat.
"They're going after the architect," he said.
"They're going after Meridian Tech Park," I corrected. "They want to destroy the Emissary."

"Same thing in their heads." Colt keyed his radio. "All units, Two-Alpha and Two-Bravo have gone rogue. They're armed and heading toward Building 7. Do NOT engage—they're still our people. But they do NOT reach the architect. I need a team to intercept."

The radio crackled with acknowledgments, but I was already processing the implications. Vance and Kowalski—two of Colt's fighters, trained, armed, and apparently convinced that destroying the architect was more important than defending the Compound. A splinter faction, born from the same distrust that Colt had expressed in our meeting and weaponized by fear.
"If they kill the Emissary, the partnership is over," I said. "The redirect stops. The infrastructure access stops. The kill switch might trigger the self-destruct protocol."
"Or it might not," Colt said. "And that's what they're betting on. Kill the alien, remove the threat, go back to fighting zombies the old-fashioned way."
"The old-fashioned way gets people killed."

"The old-fashioned way doesn't require trusting the thing that made the apocalypse." Colt met my eyes, and I saw the fracture running through him—the part that agreed with Vance fighting the part that knew better. "I'll handle my people. You handle the siege."
"You don't have enough people to do both."

"Then I'll do both anyway." He turned and headed for the Roosevelt's exit, his Command System already calculating pursuit routes and interception vectors. At the door, he paused. "Chen. If this goes wrong—if we can't stop them and they reach the Emissary—"
"I know."

"Do whatever you have to. The partnership matters. The redirect matters." The words cost him—I could see it in the tension across his shoulders. "I may not trust the architect, but I trust the math. Sixty percent reduction. That's lives. That's my people still breathing. Don't let two idiots throw that away."
Then he was gone, and I was alone with the approaching storm.
At 2:30 AM, Luna's eyes flew open.

"They've changed the plan," she gasped, grabbing my arm with a grip that left bruises. "The Omegas know about the splinter group. They're accelerating—moving now instead of at three. They're going to hit the Compound while Colt's away and we're understrength."
"How long?"
"Twenty minutes. Maybe less."

I hit the general alarm. The Compound erupted into motion—people grabbing weapons, taking positions, the controlled chaos of a group that had practiced this enough to move on instinct. But we were eight defenders now instead of sixteen, with Colt and his pursuit team racing toward Meridian Tech Park instead of holding the eastern line.

"Mia, Torres—rooftops, watch the eastern approach," I called into the radio. "Jessica, Rick, Pratika—ground floor chokepoints. Sarah, Rose, medical bay. Derek, hold the eastern stairwell. Luna, you're with me—signals only, no direct engagement unless we're breached."
The分配 was thin. Every person covered a gap that should have had two. The walls that had been designed for fifty defenders were being held by eight.
And in twenty minutes, three hundred zombies would test every inch of them.
"Contact," Mia's voice came over the radio, flat and professional. "Eastern approach. Multiple hostiles. They're not shambling—they're *sprinting*."
Sprinting. Three hundred zombies, running.
The Omega had changed the rules.
They came like a flood.

The eastern street filled with motion—bodies pouring around corners, vaulting over abandoned cars, climbing over debris with a coordination no zombie should possess. In the moonlit darkness, they looked less like a horde and more like a single organism, hundreds of limbs moving in concert, directed by an intelligence that saw us as obstacles to be overwhelmed.

Mia's first shot took down a zombie at the front of the pack. The creature behind it didn't even slow down—it stepped over the body and kept coming. The formation absorbed losses like a river absorbs stones.
"I need the Omega," Mia said. "They're too coordinated—I can't break the formation without taking out the command unit."
"Where is it?" I asked Luna.
Luna closed her eyes, wincing at the signal intensity. "Center of the formation. Protected—I can feel it, about a hundred meters back from the front line. It's using the standard zombies as shields."
"Can Mia get a shot?"
"Not from this angle. The Omegas learned from the first one—they're staying behind their forces."
"Torres, can you reposition?"
"Negative," Torres's voice came back. "The eastern building is compromised—the stairs collapsed yesterday. I can't get to a line of sight."
The horde was two hundred meters out. One hundred fifty. One hundred.
"Ground team, prepare for contact," I said. "They're going to breach the outer perimeter. Fall back to the inner chokepoints on my signal."

Jessica and Pratika—one of Colt's people who'd stayed behind—secured the ground floor doors. The Compound's architect-designed barriers were rated against Alpha-class assault, but Alpha-class meant individual zombies. This was an army.
The first wave hit the eastern barrier at 2:52 AM.

The sound was like nothing I'd ever heard—not the groan and scrape of individual zombies testing a door, but the concentrated impact of two hundred bodies driven by a single will. The barrier shuddered. Held. Shuddered again.
"It's holding!" Jessica shouted.
"It won't hold forever," I called back. "Luna—how long until they try something else?"

Luna's face was a mask of concentration, her connection to the signal allowing her to perceive the battle from a perspective none of us shared. "They're probing. Testing. The Omega is looking for weak points—it's never attacked a structure like this before. Give it time, it'll find—"
She stopped. Her eyes went wide.
"Alex. The second Omega. It's not here."
"What do you mean it's not here? You said they merged—"
"They did merge, but the second one split off. It's circling around. Coming from the *north*. It's going to hit the northern stairwell while we're focused on the eastern assault."
The northern stairwell. Where Derek was holding the door.

I was moving before the thought finished forming, Luna's axe in one hand, a fire axe from the wall in the other. The Compound's corridors scrolled past—left turn, straight, right turn, up the stairs—my body navigating by the muscle memory of six weeks of living in this place.
I reached the northern stairwell in forty seconds. Derek was there, his back against the reinforced door, his face white in the emergency lighting.

"They're coming," he said. Not confused—not anymore. His eyes were clear, focused, and for the first time since his exile, he looked like someone who understood exactly what he needed to do. "I can hear them. Dozens. Through the walls."
"The Omega is with them. A command unit—it'll try to break through."

"Then we stop it." Derek picked up a length of rebar from the barricade supplies. "I'm not asking for forgiveness. I'm not asking for trust. I'm holding this door because Sarah is in this building, and I'd rather die useful than live useless."
The door shuddered. Once. Twice.
Then the northern wall exploded inward.

Not the door—the *wall*. The Omega had directed its zombies to undermine the structural supports, sacrificing two dozen of them to create a breach. Rubble and dust filled the stairwell, and through the gap came a tide of bodies, and behind them—
The Omega.

It was different from the first one Mia had killed. This one was larger, its muscles hypertrophied, its skin stretched taut over a frame that had been rebuilt by the pathogen into something that looked like a human being reimagined by an artist who'd only had the vaguest idea of what humans were supposed to look like. Its eyes were wrong—not clouded like a standard zombie, but bright, *focusing*, moving from Derek to me to the corridor beyond with a predator's assessment.
"Alex," Luna's voice came from behind me. She'd followed. Of course she'd followed. "It can sense me. It knows what I am."

The Omega's head snapped toward Luna. Something crossed its distorted features—recognition, perhaps, or hunger. It made a sound that wasn't a growl or a word but something in between, a vibration that I felt in my bones.

Luna staggered. Her hand went to her head, and she cried out—not in pain, but in *resonance*. The Omega was broadcasting directly at her, its signal overwhelming her senses like a foghorn in a library.
"Luna!" I grabbed her shoulder, anchoring her.

"I'm—okay—" She wasn't okay. Her eyes were dilated, her breathing ragged. But she was still standing, still fighting, still *choosing* to be present. "It's trying to take over. To bring me into the network. A receiver should be a transceiver—"
"You are NOT joining that thing."
"I won't." Her jaw set. "But it's learning my frequency. Every second we fight, it understands me better."
The Omega took a step forward. Behind it, the zombies poured through the breach, filling the stairwell, a wall of grasping hands and snapping teeth between us and the creature that commanded them.
Derek stepped into the gap.

Not to fight—all he had was a length of rebar, and the Omega could tear him apart without slowing down. He stepped into the gap to *hold it*, to buy time, to be the obstacle that let the rest of us survive.
"Go," he said, not looking back. "Get her out of here. Get everyone out."
"Derek—"
"I owe this." His voice was steady. Calm. The calm of a man who'd made peace with his endpoint. "I owe Sarah. I owe all of you. And this is how I pay."

The Omega charged. Derek met it with the rebar—a pitiful weapon against something that wasn't human anymore. The impact rang through the corridor like a bell, and Derek was thrown backward, but he didn't stop. He got up. He charged again.
He was buying us minutes. That was all.
I grabbed Luna and ran.

**THREAT UPDATE: COMPOUND BREACHED — NORTHERN WALL**
**SECOND OMEGA ENGAGING ON NORTH FRONT**
**DEREK VOSS — HOLDING ACTION (CRITICAL)**
**EVACUATION TO INNER DEFENSES IN PROGRESS**
